title: Surgery (including Allied Disciplines)

prerequisites.text: Successful completion of all co urses within the preceding academic year.

outline: Objective: The General Surgery programme comprises an 8-week 5th-year course and a 4-week 6th- year course which aim to equip generalist health professionals w ith the ability to provide safe emergency and primary-level surgical care. The objective of the 6th year course is applying practical diagnostic, management, and procedural skills as student interns. By the end of the course, students will be able to: (a) Describe the pr inciples of surgical care at dist rict and regional hospital level, including patient prioritisation, es calation pathways, referral sy stems, and the management of common surgical conditions, (b) Di scuss the sociopolitical determin ants of health and systemic barriers to accessing surgical care in South Afri ca, and advocate for equita ble access to essential surgical services, (c) Appl y clinical reasoning to as sess patients, interpret clinical data, construct differential diagnoses, and form ulate safe, appropriate managemen t plans for common surgical presentations, (d) Demonstrate safe and effective technique in essential surgical procedures, including wound care, suturing, drain placement, wound debridement, and assisting in theatre under supervision, (e) Participate actively an d responsibly as a member of the surgical team, including contribution to ward rounds, patient admissions, operative lists, after-hours ca lls, effective communication with patients and the healthcare team, and accurate completion of clinical records and logbook, (f) Engage DEPARTMENTS IN THE FACULTY 249 in self-directed learning and conti nuous self-evaluation, using clinical experie nces and feedback to identify areas for improvement and consolidate learning. Course outline: The 6th-year General Surgery cou rse follows a workplace-based, apprenticeship-style learning approach, focusing on self-direct ed learning and clinical immersio n. Students rece ive a practical handbook on managing common surgical resentations and are allocated to either Victoria, Mitchell’s Plain, New Somerset or Groote Schuur Hospital where they have exposure to common surgical conditions and procedures. They are integrated into the General Surgery team and are involved in all aspects of the unit’s activities. Students take responsibility for patients, accompanying them to procedures and present on calls, ward rounds, and at unit meetings. Students are expected to perform 4 calls (3 weekday and 1 weekend) during the 4-we ek rotation. Supervisors will provide a weekly interactive case-based tutorial. Students compile a Clinical Exposure Logbook documenting portfolio cases, calls, and tutorial attend ance. In addition to clinical ex posure, the department hosts two compulsory workshops: (a) Pathways to Surgical Care: joint workshop (between Global Surgery, General Surgery, and Obstetrics) that runs every 8 weeks with sessions scheduled on a Friday morning from 08h00 to 12h30. The aim is to develop understanding about socioeconomic inequity in accessing surgical care, the pathways to surgical care, an d essential advocacy compet encies, (b) Suturing and Wound Care: this workshop happens after the orientation. Students will revise knot-tying and suturing and practice wound debridement and drain placement.
lecture_times: See module block schedule and official class timetable.
dp_requirements: All scheduled activities are considered compulsory. DP for the General Surgery course is granted for: (a) Atte ndance at Suturing and Wound Care wo rkshop. Students will sign the register. Attendance at Pathways to Surgical Care workshop. Stude nts will sign the register, (b) Attendance at scheduled clinical activities. Clinical supervisor will sign the students’ rotation logbook daily. DP withheld if the student misses more than 3 signatures in total without CMC, (c) Completion of Clinical Exposure Logbook. St udents must clerk and log a mini mum of 6 patients. This logbook must be handed in at the end-of-block examination, (d) Completion of end-of-block assessments. See breakdown under ‘Assessments’. Absence up to one week (including concession to miss class) may require the student to attend supp lementary clinical time. Absenc e exceeding one week (including concession to miss class) will require the student to repeat the course. Should it become apparent that a student has signed a register/ obtained a signature but not attended the session, the case is reported to the Academic Misconduct Committee.
assessment: The General Surgery mark comprises three assessments. Students must obtain 50% for each component (sub-minimum) and a weighted overall average of 50% to pass. Students obtaining an average of between 48% and 50% will be recommended to complete a supplementary examination. Students obtaining less than 48% will be required to repeat the surgical block. (a) Clinical Examination – weighted 33.33% [1/3] During the last week of the rotation, students will clerk and examine a patient with a common surgical condition at their allocated hosp ital. They will presen t their findings to an examiner and discuss the investig ation and management appropriat e to the patient. (b) Oral examination – weighted 33.33% [1/3] On the last Friday of the rotation, students will be examined by means of oral questioning and a standardized rubric on two common surgical presentations. (c) Multiple-Choice Question (MCQ) Exam ination – weighted 33 .33% [1/3] On the last Friday of the rotation, students will have a 2- hour computer-based Multiple-Choice Question (MCQ) comprising 27 three-part questions based on a clinical scenario testing clini cal reasoning ability. Students may NOT go back to previous questi ons and there is no negative mark ing. Should a student obtain an overall pass mark but fail one or more sub-components, then they will be r ecommended to attend supplementary clinical time and complete a supplementary assessment. 1% is awarded as extra credit to students completing the end-of-block evaluation form.
